questions.question: Explain the RGB color model and how colors are represented in digital displays. Give an example of how yellow is represented.
keyPoints: RGB is an additive color model using Red, Green, Blue as primary colors
Colors are created by combining different intensities of each primary
Each channel typically uses 8 bits (0-255)
Yellow = Red(255) + Green(255) + Blue(0)
Higher values mean more light intensity

questions.question: What is the difference between additive and subtractive color models? Where is each used?
keyPoints: Additive adds light - used in displays (monitors, phones)
Subtractive absorbs light - used in printing
RGB is additive: combining all primaries makes white
CMY is subtractive: combining all makes black
CMYK adds black (K) for better print quality

questions.question: Define keyframes and tweening in animation. Why are they important?
keyPoints: Keyframes are specific frames that define start/end points of animation
Tweening (interpolation) generates intermediate frames automatically
Reduces manual work - animator only creates key poses
Allows smoother, more complex animations
Types include linear, ease-in, ease-out interpolation

questions.question: What is color depth? Calculate the number of colors possible in a 24-bit color system.
keyPoints: Color depth = bits used to represent color per pixel
24-bit color uses 8 bits for each R, G, B channel
Each channel has 256 possible values (0-255)
Also called 'true color' - nearly matches human color perception

questions.question: Explain the concept of frame rate (fps) in animation. What is the standard fps for cinema and why?
keyPoints: Frame rate = number of frames displayed per second
Standard cinema = 24 fps (established in 1927)
Higher fps = smoother motion but more data/processing
30 fps common for TV, 60 fps for gaming
Human eye perceives smooth motion above ~24 fps

questions.question: What is alpha blending? How does the RGBA color model work?
keyPoints: Alpha channel represents transparency (0-255)
RGBA adds 4th byte to RGB for 32-bit color
Alpha 0 = fully transparent, 255 = fully opaque
Allows layering and compositing of images
Useful for special effects, UI design, image editing

questions.question: Describe the HSV color model and its components.
keyPoints: HSV stands for Hue, Saturation, Value
Hue = color type (0-360 degrees on color wheel)
Saturation = color intensity/purity (0-100%)
Value = brightness of color (0-100%)
More intuitive for humans to select colors

questions.question: Explain the complete process of creating a computer animation from concept to final output. Discuss keyframes, interpolation, timing, and rendering.
keyPoints: Animation begins with storyboard and design
Define keyframes at important poses (start, end, major changes)
Computer calculates in-between frames using tweening/interpolation
Apply easing functions for natural motion (ease-in, ease-out)
Set frame rate and timing for smoothness
Add layers, effects, and compositing
Final rendering produces sequence of images or video file
Key considerations: motion blur, anti-aliasing, color management

questions.question: Explain color representation in computer graphics. Discuss RGB, CMY, and HSV models with their applications, color depth, and how displays render colors.
keyPoints: RGB - additive model for self-luminous displays (monitors, phones)
CMY - subtractive model for printing (adds black as CMYK)
HSV - intuitive model based on human perception
Color depth determines range of colors (8-bit, 24-bit, 32-bit)
Displays use pixels with RGB sub-pixels
Each pixel's RGB values determine color shown
Higher color depth = more colors = more memory required
Color management ensures consistency across devices

questions.question: Discuss animation principles and techniques. Include keyframing, tweening methods, easing functions, and factors affecting animation quality.
keyPoints: Keyframing defines critical poses; computer interpolates between them
Tweening types: linear (constant speed), ease-in (accelerate), ease-out (decelerate)
Animation principles: squash & stretch, anticipation, staging, timing
Frame rate affects smoothness - higher is smoother but needs more processing
Resolution impacts detail - higher resolution needs more memory
Anti-aliasing smooths edges for better quality
Motion blur adds realism by showing movement trails
Rendering converts animation to final video/file format

questions.question: Calculate and explain: (a) Number of colors in 16-bit color (5-5-5 format), (b) Memory required for 1920×1080 image at 32-bit color, (c) Data rate for 60fps 1080p video at 24-bit color.
keyPoints: (a) 5 bits per channel = 32×32×32 = 32,768 colors
(c) Per frame: 6.2 MB; Per second at 60fps: 371 MB/s
Compression typically reduces these requirements significantly
Bit rate formulas: width × height × bits per pixel × fps
Storage considerations important for video production
